summary: Julia Evans shares progress on her TCP stack implementation, describing a key insight from TCP/IP Illustrated: that every TCP packet except the initial SYN can have the ACK flag set. This realization significantly simplified her packet-sending code. She's taking a correctness-first approach by ACKing every received packet, and has started thinking about implementing bind() for server-side TCP, which turns out to be surprisingly complex. She also celebrates how tests enabled a major refactoring.
key_points: Every TCP packet except the initial SYN can have the ACK flag set, which dramatically simplifies packet construction code
A correctness-first approach (ACKing every packet) is a valid strategy even if it's not efficient
The bind() syscall is deceptively complex—it must manage multiple connections and queuing simultaneously
Tests enabled a confident large-scale refactoring of the TCP stack code
money_quotes: I realized I can construct basically every packet the same way, except for the flags and the payload.
Right now the way I'm handling ACKs is basically to send an ACK for every packet I receive. This isn't really efficient... But it is not incorrect, which is all that I'm going for.
Seriously I did not ever think about how bind() works before. It has to manage multiple connections! And keep a queue!
What am I supposed to do when people send me packets all at once? Just ignore them until I have time? I don't get it.
The code still works, because tests. =D
key_insight: A correctness-first approach—doing the simple, technically valid thing before optimizing—is a powerful strategy when building complex protocol implementations from scratch.
